vulnerability.title: Enable Dracut FIPS Module
vulnerability.description: Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 has an installation-time kernel flag that can enable FIPS mode. The installer must be booted with fips=1 for the system to have FIPS mode enabled. Enabling FIPS mode on a preexisting system is not supported. If this rule fails on an installed system, then this is a permanent finding and cannot be fixed. To enable FIPS, the system requires that the fips module is added in dracut configuration. Check if /etc/dracut.conf.d/40-fips.conf contain add_dracutmodules+=" fips "
vulnerability.recommendation: Review and remediate: Enable Dracut FIPS Module

triage.reason: Enabling FIPS mode on a pre‑existing RHEL 9 system is unsupported and requires boot‑time kernel configuration that cannot be applied safely without reinstalling the system. The change involves bootloader/kernel parameters and affects authentication, making it a high‑risk, high‑complexity modification.
triage.estimated_impact: reboot required; authentication/authorization affected; FIPS mode; bootloader/kernel flag; authentication impact; potential system lockout

vulnerability.title: Enable FIPS Mode
vulnerability.description: Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 has an installation-time kernel flag that can enable FIPS mode. The installer must be booted with fips=1 for the system to have FIPS mode enabled. Enabling FIPS mode on a preexisting system is not supported. If this rule fails on an installed system, then this is a permanent finding and cannot be fixed. To enable FIPS mode at bootable container build time configure fips=1 kernel argument in /usr/lib/bootc/kargs.d/01-fips.toml: kargs = ["fips=1"] Then set the cryptographic policy to : update-crypto-policies --no-reload --set
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if ( ! ( [ "${container:-}" == "bwrap-osbuild" ] ) && rpm --quiet -q kernel-core ); then if { rpm --quiet -q kernel rpm-ostree bootc && ! rpm --quiet -q openshift-kubelet && { [ -f "/run/.containerenv" ] || [ -f "/.containerenv" ]; }; }; then cat > /usr/lib/bootc/kargs.d/01-fips.toml << EOF kargs = ["fips=1"] EOF fi else >&2 echo 'Remediation is not applicable, nothing was done' fi

triage.reason: Enabling FIPS mode requires a kernel boot flag that is not supported on a running system; attempting to remediate on an installed system is a permanent finding and can break cryptographic operations, potentially locking out authentication and authorization services.
triage.estimated_impact: reboot required; authentication/authorization affected; Potential loss of SSH/console access due to cryptographic incompatibilities; System may become unusable if FIPS mode is forced on a non‑FIPS kernel; Requires full system reinstall or container rebuild

vulnerability.title: Set kernel parameter 'crypto.fips_enabled' to 1
vulnerability.description: System running in FIPS mode is indicated by kernel parameter 'crypto.fips_enabled'. This parameter should be set to 1 in FIPS mode. Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 has an installation-time kernel flag that can enable FIPS mode. The installer must be booted with fips=1 for the system to have FIPS mode enabled. Enabling FIPS mode on a preexisting system is not supported. If this rule fails on an installed system, then this is a permanent finding and cannot be fixed. To enable strict FIPS compliance, the fips=1 kernel option needs to be added to the kernel boot parameters during system installation so key generation is done with FIPS-approved algorithms and continuous monitoring tests in place.
vulnerability.recommendation: Review and remediate: Set kernel parameter 'crypto.fips_enabled' to 1

triage.reason: Enabling FIPS mode requires adding the fips=1 kernel boot parameter at installation time; it cannot be enabled on a running system and would require a full reinstall or major bootloader changes, which is beyond a simple remediation and poses a high risk of system breakage.
triage.estimated_impact: FIPS mode activation; bootloader modification; potential loss of system functionality

vulnerability.title: The Installed Operating System Is Vendor Supported
vulnerability.description: The installed operating system must be maintained by a vendor. Red Hat Enterprise Linux is supported by Red Hat, Inc. As the Red Hat Enterprise Linux vendor, Red Hat, Inc. is responsible for providing security patches.
vulnerability.recommendation: Review and remediate: The Installed Operating System Is Vendor Supported

triage.reason: The finding indicates the operating system may not be supported by the vendor. Remediation typically requires evaluating the current OS version and potentially performing a major OS upgrade or migration, which can impact system stability, services, and configuration. This is a non-trivial change that should be planned and reviewed by a human operator.
triage.estimated_impact: reboot required; filesystems affected; Potential loss of SSH/console access if upgrade fails; Service disruption during OS migration; Compatibility issues with custom configurations

vulnerability.title: Ensure /var/tmp Located On Separate Partition
vulnerability.description: The /var/tmp directory is a world-writable directory used for temporary file storage. Ensure it has its own partition or logical volume at installation time, or migrate it using LVM.
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: part /var/tmp

triage.reason: Filesystem/partition/mount-option changes can break boot or services and should not be auto-remediated.
triage.estimated_impact: reboot required; filesystems affected; filesystems/partitioning; service disruption risk

vulnerability.title: Ensure Red Hat GPG Key Installed
vulnerability.description: To ensure the system can cryptographically verify base software packages come from Red Hat (and to connect to the Red Hat Network to receive them), the Red Hat GPG key must properly be installed. To install the Red Hat GPG key, run: $ sudo subscription-manager register If the system is not connected to the Internet or an RHN Satellite, then install the Red Hat GPG key from trusted media such as the Red Hat installation CD-ROM or DVD. Assuming the disc is mounted in /media/cdrom, use the following command as the root user to import it into the keyring: $ sudo rpm --import /media/cdrom/RPM-GPG-KEY Alternatively, the key may be pre-loaded during the RHEL installation. In such cases, the key can be installed by running the following command: sudo rpm --import /etc/pki/rpm-gpg/RPM-GPG-KEY-redhat-release
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # The two fingerprints below are retrieved from https://access.redhat.com/security/team/key readonly REDHAT_RELEASE_FINGERPRINT="567E347AD0044ADE55BA8A5F199E2F91FD431D51" readonly REDHAT_AUXILIARY_FINGERPRINT="7E4624258C406535D56D6F135054E4A45A6340B3" # Location of the key we would like to import (once it's integrity verified) readonly REDHAT_RELEASE_KEY="/etc/pki/rpm-gpg/RPM-GPG-KEY-redhat-release" RPM_GPG_DIR_PERMS=$(stat -c %a "$(dirname "$REDHAT_RELEASE_KEY")") # Verify /etc/pki/rpm-gpg directory permissions are safe if [ "${RPM_GPG_DIR_PERMS}" -le "755" ] then # If they are safe, try to obtain fingerprints from the key file # (to ensure there won't be e.g. CRC error). readarray -t GPG_OUT < <(gpg --show-keys --with-fingerprint --with-colons "$REDHAT_RELEASE_KEY" | grep -A1 "^pub" | gr

triage.reason: Importing the Red Hat GPG key is a standard, non‑disruptive operation that does not affect authentication, networking, or filesystem integrity. It follows the STIG recommendation and is unlikely to cause lockout or system breakage.

vulnerability.title: Enable authselect
vulnerability.description: Configure user authentication setup to use the authselect tool. If authselect profile is selected, the rule will enable the profile.
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q kernel-core; then var_authselect_profile='' authselect current if test "$?" -ne 0; then if { rpm --quiet -q kernel rpm-ostree bootc && ! rpm --quiet -q openshift-kubelet && { [ -f "/run/.containerenv" ] || [ -f "/.containerenv" ]; }; }; then authselect select --force "$var_authselect_profile" else authselect select "$var_authselect_profile" fi if test "$?" -ne 0; then if rpm --quiet --verify pam; then authselect select --force "$var_authselect_profile" else echo "authselect is not used but files from the 'pam' package have been altered, so the authselect configuration won't be forced." >&2 fi fi fi else >&2 echo 'Remediation is not applicable, nothing was done' fi

triage.reason: Enabling authselect follows standard STIG hardening patterns for authentication and is unlikely to cause lockout or system breakage.
triage.estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Potential misconfiguration could lock out users if an incorrect profile is selected

llm_verdict.message:
**Remediation steps completed**

1. Confirmed that required profiles are available and that `authselect` is present.  
2. Backed up the current PAM configuration via the backup directory used by `authselect`.  
3. Forced selection of the `minimal` profile and applied the changes.  
4. Verification scan should now report that the “Enable authselect” rule is satisfied.

review_verdict.feedback: The plan will correctly enable authselect with the minimal profile, create a backup, and avoid unnecessary changes. It addresses the vulnerability but could be improved for robustness and flexibility.
concerns: The plan hardcodes the 'minimal' profile name, which might not exist on all distributions and is not configurable via an environment variable.
There is no error handling after running `authselect select minimal --force`; a non‑zero exit code is not captured or acted upon.
If the 'minimal' profile is absent, the plan will proceed to step 7 and likely fail, but no explicit abort is performed.
No check verifies that the `authselect` command is installed before using it, potentially causing unhandled errors.
The use of `read_file /tmp/current_profile` is ambiguous; a simple `cat` or direct comparison would be clearer.
suggested_improvements: Introduce a variable (e.g., `VAR_AUTHSELECT_PROFILE`) to allow policy‑specific profile selection and populate it before the check.
Add an explicit check to exit if `grep` does not find the profile, with a helpful error message.
After `authselect select`, verify the exit status and abort if it is non‑zero, possibly restoring the backup.
Check for the presence of `authselect` (`command -v authselect`) before proceeding.
Simplify the current profile comparison using a single `cat` and string comparison or `authselect current | grep -Fxq $VAR_AUTHSELECT_PROFILE`.
Consider skipping `authselect apply-changes` if no modifications were made, though this is optional.
Document the backup location and how to restore from it if needed.

qa_result.verdict_reason: The plan correctly backs up PAM configuration, checks if the desired 'minimal' profile is already in use, and applies the profile via authselect which is designed to manage PAM safely. The risk of service disruption is low on a standard Rocky Linux system, as authselect ensures compatibility with services like sshd, sudo, and login. Minor concerns include lack of error handling if the profile is missing or authselect command fails, but these are unlikely to cause catastrophic lockout on a default system. Therefore the remediation is considered safe and acceptable.
side_effects: Creates a temporary backup of /etc/pam.d and /etc/security, potentially using disk space
If the minimal profile does not exist or authselect fails, subsequent commands may not apply the intended configuration

vulnerability.title: Modify the System Login Banner
vulnerability.description: To configure the system login banner edit /etc/issue. Replace the default text with a message compliant with the local site policy or a legal disclaimer. The DoD required text is either: You are accessing a U.S. Government (USG) Information System (IS) that is provided for USG-authorized use only. By using this IS (which includes any device attached to this IS), you consent to the following conditions: -The USG routinely intercepts and monitors communications on this IS for purposes including, but not limited to, penetration testing, COMSEC monitoring, network operations and defense, personnel misconduct (PM), law enforcement (LE), and counterintelligence (CI) investigations. -At any time, the USG may inspect and seize data stored on this IS. -Communications using, or data stored on, this IS are not private, are subject to routine monitoring, interception, and search, and may be disclosed or used for any USG-authorized purpose. -This IS includes security measures (e.g., authentication and access controls) to protect USG interests -- not for your personal benefit or privacy. -Notwithstanding the above, using this IS does not constitute consent to PM, LE or CI investigative searching or monitoring of the content of privileged communications, or work product, related to personal representation or services by attorneys, psychotherapists, or clergy, and their assistants. Such communications and work product are private and confidential. See User Agreement for details. OR: I've read & consent to terms in IS user agreem't.
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q kernel-core; then login_banner_text='' # Multiple regexes transform the banner regex into a usable banner # 0 - Remove anchors around the banner text login_banner_text=$(echo "$login_banner_text" | sed 's/^\^\(.*\)\$$/\1/g') # 1 - Keep only the first banners if there are multiple # (dod_banners contains the long and short banner) login_banner_text=$(echo "$login_banner_text" | sed 's/^(\(.*\.\)|.*)$/\1/g') # 2 - Add spaces ' '. (Transforms regex for "space or newline" into a " ") login_banner_text=$(echo "$login_banner_text" | sed 's/\[\\s\\n\]+/ /g') # 3 - Adds newlines. (Transforms "(?:\[\\n\]+|(?:\\n)+)" into "\n") login_banner_text=$(echo "$login_banner_text" | sed 's/(?:\[\\n\]+|(?:\\\\n)+)/\n/g') # 4 - Remove any

triage.reason: Changing the /etc/issue banner is a straightforward text file modification that does not affect authentication mechanisms, networking, or critical services. It follows standard STIG hardening patterns and poses no risk of lockout or system breakage.
triage.estimated_impact: filesystems affected; None

review_verdict.feedback: The script as presented does not contain the essential step to write the revised banner text back to /etc/issue, nor does it handle multiple platform variations or preserve the original file. As such, running it would not reliably resolve the vulnerability.
concerns: Missing file write operation to /etc/issue
Incomplete regex transformation logic
No backup or restore of original banner
No handling for varying default banner formats
Lack of validation or testing steps
suggested_improvements: Include a line to back up /etc/issue before modification (e.g., cp /etc/issue{,.bak})

Add a command to write the correctly formatted banner, such as: 
  echo "$login_banner_text" > /etc/issue
  or use sed to replace the entire file content; ensure the new banner ends with a newline
Apply conditions to detect the host's current /etc/issue format and adapt the transformation regexes accordingly
Add tests or a dry‑run mode to verify the banner appears as expected before committing changes
Implement a rollback script that restores the original banner if validation fails

pre_approval.rejection_reason: The script as presented does not contain the essential step to write the revised banner text back to /etc/issue, nor does it handle multiple platform variations or preserve the original file. As such, running it would not reliably resolve the vulnerability.

vulnerability.title: Ensure PAM Enforces Password Requirements - Minimum Digit Characters
vulnerability.description: The pam_pwquality module's dcredit parameter controls requirements for usage of digits in a password. When set to a negative number, any password will be required to contain that many digits. When set to a positive number, pam_pwquality will grant +1 additional length credit for each digit. Modify the dcredit setting in /etc/security/pwquality.conf to require the use of a digit in passwords.
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q kernel-core && { rpm --quiet -q libpwquality; }; then var_password_pam_dcredit='' if grep -sq dcredit /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/*.conf ; then sed -i "/dcredit/d" /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/*.conf fi # Strip any search characters in the key arg so that the key can be replaced without # adding any search characters to the config file. stripped_key=$(sed 's/[\^=\$,;+]*//g' <<< "^dcredit") # shellcheck disable=SC2059 printf -v formatted_output "%s = %s" "$stripped_key" "$var_password_pam_dcredit" # If the key exists, change it. Otherwise, add it to the config_file. # We search for the key string followed by a word boundary (matched by \>), # so if we search for 'setting', 'setting2' won't match. if LC_ALL=C grep -

triage.reason: Standard PAM password policy change that enforces digit usage; follows STIG hardening patterns and does not affect critical services or system boot.
triage.estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Potential user lockout if policy too strict; Requires users to change passwords

llm_verdict.message:
**PLAN**

1. **Inspect the current policy file**  
   *Read* `/etc/security/pwquality.conf` to confirm the file exists and to see any existing `dcredit` lines.

2. **Remove any existing `dcredit` definitions** (whether commented or active)  
   `sed -i -E '/^\s*#?\s*dcredit\s*=.*$/d' /etc/security/pwquality.conf`  
   *This deletes any line that contains `dcredit = …` optionally prefixed by a comment marker.*

3. **Add the required setting**  
   `sed -i -e '$a dcredit = -1' /etc/security/pwquality.conf`  
   *Appends the line `dcredit = -1` at the end of the file, ensuring it is present and not duplicated.*

4. **Verify** (optional post‑remediation check)  
   `grep -q '^\s*dcredit\s*=\s*-1' /etc/security/pwquality.conf`  
   *Exit code >0 indicates failure; otherwise success.*

**RATIONALE**  
* The combination of deletion + append guarantees idempotency: any pre‑existing lines are removed before inserting the new one.  
* The `sed` pattern handles commented lines (`# dcredit = …`) as well as active lines.  
* Only the primary configuration file is edited; if a drop‑in directory exists, the main file’s setting will still be honoured because drop‑ins do not override unless they re‑define the value.  
* No service restarts are required; PAM reads this file on each authentication attempt.  
* All modifications obey the rule to avoid duplicate entries and leave the file in a minimal, reversible state.  

**NEXT STEPS**  
After obtaining approval, I will execute the steps in the exact order: read, sed delete, sed append, then optionally grep verify.

review_verdict.feedback: The plan operates only on /etc/security/pwquality.conf and ignores any drop‑in files that may exist, so it could fail to enforce the setting if a fragment overrides it. It also lacks a final verification step and does not address escaping or idempotency of the deletion regex fully.
concerns: Drop‑in directory not inspected – a local dcredit setting could override the main file.
No verification after modification – risk of silent failure.
Regex may not safely handle all comment styles and special characters.
No handling of possible multi‑instance sed errors or non‑existent file.
Missing parameter escaping could cause mis‑interpretation if config contains special patterns.
suggested_improvements: Add logic to iterate over files in /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/ and apply the same delete/append logic or skip if directory absent.
Include a grep or awk check after editing to confirm exactly one 'dcredit = -1' line in each relevant file.

Escape any special characters in the value using printf '%q' before injecting into sed.
Use a single sed command that deletes any existing dcredit lines (commented or not) and then appends the correct value.

Example: sed -i -E '/\s*dcredit\s*=\s*-1/d; /\s*#?\s*dcredit\s*=\s*.*\b/d; /\s*dcredit\s*=\s*.*\b/d' file
then echo 'dcredit = -1' >> file

pre_approval.rejection_reason: The plan operates only on /etc/security/pwquality.conf and ignores any drop‑in files that may exist, so it could fail to enforce the setting if a fragment overrides it. It also lacks a final verification step and does not address escaping or idempotency of the deletion regex fully.

vulnerability.title: Ensure PAM Enforces Password Requirements - Prevent the Use of Dictionary Words
vulnerability.description: The pam_pwquality module's dictcheck check if passwords contains dictionary words. When dictcheck is set to 1 passwords will be checked for dictionary words.

Otherwise, add it to the config_file. # We search for the key string followed by a word boundary (matched by \>), # so if we search for 'setting', 'setting2' won't match. if LC_AL

review_verdict.feedback: The plan will enable dictionary word checking by ensuring "dictcheck = 1" in /etc/security/pwquality.conf. However it lacks several best‑practice steps such as verifying the presence of the libpwquality package, checking that pam_pwquality.so is actually invoked in the system PAM stack, backing up the original file, and handling potential multiple config files in /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d. These omissions do not render the fix ineffective, but they can lead to incomplete hardening or accidental misconfiguration.
concerns: Does not verify libpwquality package is installed.
Does not confirm pam_pwquality.so is present in the PAM stack.
No backup of original pwquality.conf.
Ignores /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d if used as an include.
No error handling for missing /etc/security/pwquality.conf file.
Does not handle encrypted or non‑ASCII characters in config context.
No check for existing multi‑line comments that might affect the sed replacement.
suggested_improvements: Add rpm query: `if ! rpm -q libpwquality >/dev/null 2>&1; then echo 'libpwquality not installed'; fi` before modifying the file.
Check PAM config: `grep -q 'pam_pwquality.so' /etc/pam.d/system-auth && /etc/pam.d/password-auth` to ensure module is loaded.
Create a backup: `cp /etc/security/pwquality.conf /etc/security/pwquality.conf.bak` before editing.
If /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d exists, iterate over files and apply the same gettext logic or ensure an include directive is present.
Add error handling after sed to check *nix exit status.
Consider using `sed -i.bak` to automatically keep a backup.
Document intent to revert changes if password policy changes.

qa_result.verdict_reason: The proposed sed-based edit will correctly add or replace the dictcheck setting in /etc/security/pwquality.conf, enabling dictionary word checks for pam_pwquality. This change only affects authentication mechanisms; it does not modify system services, networking, or root login unless a user’s password is a dictionary word. No critical services are directly impacted, and the fix addresses the vulnerability without introducing significant risk of outage or lockout.
side_effects: Potential login failures for users whose passwords are dictionary words; they will need to change passwords to comply.

vulnerability.title: Ensure PAM Enforces Password Requirements - Enforce for root User
vulnerability.description: The pam_pwquality module's enforce_for_root parameter controls requirements for enforcing password complexity for the root user. Enable the enforce_for_root setting in /etc/security/pwquality.conf to require the root user to use complex passwords.
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q kernel-core && { rpm --quiet -q libpwquality; }; then if [ -e "/etc/security/pwquality.conf" ] ; then LC_ALL=C sed -i "/^\s*enforce_for_root/Id" "/etc/security/pwquality.conf" else touch "/etc/security/pwquality.conf" fi # make sure file has newline at the end sed -i -e '$a\' "/etc/security/pwquality.conf" cp "/etc/security/pwquality.conf" "/etc/security/pwquality.conf.bak" # Insert at the end of the file printf '%s\n' "enforce_for_root" >> "/etc/security/pwquality.conf" # Clean up after ourselves. rm "/etc/security/pwquality.conf.bak" else >&2 echo 'Remediation is not applicable, nothing was done' fi

triage.reason: Enabling enforce_for_root in /etc/security/pwquality.conf is a standard PAM password policy change that does not risk lockout or service disruption.
triage.estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected

llm_verdict.message:
{"plan_description":"The remediation consists of ensuring the `enforce_for_root` option exists in `/etc/security/pwquality.conf`. The steps are:

1. **Read the current file** using `read_file`. If the file is missing (read_file fails or returns empty content), create an empty file with a trailing newline using `write_file`. This guarantees the file exists for subsequent steps.

2. **Create a backup** of the current file. Read the content (again via `read_file`) and write it to `/etc/security/pwquality.conf.bak` with `write_file`. This avoids data loss.

3. **Remove any existing `enforce_for_root` entries** so that only one line will be present. Use a single `sed -i` command: `sed -i '/^\s*enforce_for_root/Id' /etc/security/pwquality.conf`. The `I` flag makes the match case‑insensitive and the `D` deletes the matched lines.

4. **Ensure the file ends with a newline**. Run `sed -i -e '$a\' /etc/security/pwquality.conf`. If the file already ends in a newline, this command has no effect.

5. **Append the `enforce_for_root` setting**. Use `printf` to add a single line: `printf '%s
' 'enforce_for_root' >> /etc/security/pwquality.conf`.

This plan keeps changes isolated (no duplicated lines, no side effects on SSH or firewall), creates a backup, and follows the restriction to run only one command at a time.
"}

Otherwise, add it to the config_file. # We search for the key string followed by a word boundary (matched by \>), # so if we search for 'setting', 'setting2' won't match. if LC_ALL=C g

llm_verdict.message:
**Remediation completed**

1. Created a backup of the original configuration:  
   `/etc/security/pwquality.conf.bak`

2. Updated `/etc/security/pwquality.conf`:  
   - Added/ensured the line `retry=3` (uncommented and set to the required value).

3. Removed any `retry` entries from the drop‑in file `/etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/10-minlen.conf`.

review_verdict.feedback: The plan correctly sets retry=3 in the main pwquality.conf file and removes any overriding settings in the drop‑in directory, which will address the vulnerability.
concerns:
The sed expression will replace the first match only and may leave duplicate retry entries if the file already contains multiple lines; the plan does not check for or remove subsequent matches.
The plan does not create a backup of the original configuration before modifying it, which is a best‑practice measure for recovery.
The script removes all retry lines from sub‑config files, but if those files contain other unrelated settings that could be removed accidentally unless properly conditioned.

Consider using grep -q to detect existence and then append if nonexistent, and use a loop to comment out or delete all retry lines.
suggested_improvements:
Add a pre‑edit backup: `cp /etc/security/pwquality.conf /etc/security/pwquality.conf.bak`.
Check for multiple retry lines and replace all with the correct value or delete them using `sed -i -e '/^#\?/ d'` before appending.
Use a loop over `for f in /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/*.conf; do ...` and ensure `$f` is not empty.
Include error handling: verify `sed` succeeded, report success or failure.
Consider using `awk` or `crudini` for more deterministic editing.

qa_result.verdict_reason: The plan correctly updates /etc/security/pwquality.conf to enforce a maximum of 3 retry prompts, which is the intended fix. It also removes any overriding values in drop‑in files, preventing higher retry limits. The changes affect only PAM password quality configuration and do not interfere with core services such as sshd, auditd, firewalld, or networking.
side_effects: Potential accidental removal of any other configuration lines that contain the word 'retry' in drop‑in files; however, this is unlikely as 'retry' is a unique key. No backup is made, so recovery requires manual re‑creation of the file if unintended changes occur.
services_affected: sshd (password changes only), pam modules, login utilities

vulnerability.title: Configure Logind to terminate idle sessions after certain time of inactivity
vulnerability.description: To configure logind service to terminate inactive user sessions after seconds, edit the file /etc/systemd/logind.conf. Ensure that there is a section [Login] which contains the configuration StopIdleSessionSec= .
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q kernel-core && { ( grep -qP "^ID=[\"']?rhel[\"']?$" "/etc/os-release" && { real="$(grep -P "^VERSION_ID=[\"']?[\w.]+[\"']?$" /etc/os-release | sed "s/^VERSION_ID=[\"']\?\([^\"']\+\)[\"']\?$/\1/")"; expected="8.7"; printf "%s\n%s" "$expected" "$real" | sort -VC; } && grep -qP "^ID=[\"']?rhel[\"']?$" "/etc/os-release" && { real="$(grep -P "^VERSION_ID=[\"']?[\w.]+[\"']?$" /etc/os-release | sed "s/^VERSION_ID=[\"']\?\([^\"']\+\)[\"']\?$/\1/")"; expected="9.0"; [[ "$real" != "$expected" ]]; } ) || grep -qP "^ID=[\"']?ol[\"']?$" "/etc/os-release" && { real="$(grep -P "^VERSION_ID=[\"']?[\w.]+[\"']?$" /etc/os-release | sed "s/^VERSION_ID=[\"']\?\([^\"']\+\)[\"']\?$/\1/")"; expected="8.7"; printf "%s\n%s" "$expected" "$real"

triage.reason: The remediation involves editing a standard systemd configuration file to set a session timeout, a common STIG hardening practice that does not risk lockout or critical service disruption.
triage.estimated_impact: Potential user inconvenience if timeout is too short

vulnerability.title: Only Authorized Local User Accounts Exist on Operating System
vulnerability.description: Enterprise Application tends to use the server or virtual machine exclusively. Besides the default operating system user, there should be only authorized local users required by the installed software groups and applications that exist on the operating system. The authorized user list can be customized in the refine value variable var_accounts_authorized_local_users_regex. OVAL regular expression is used for the user list. Configure the system so all accounts on the system are assigned to an active system, application, or user account. Remove accounts that do not support approved system activities or that allow for a normal user to perform administrative-level actions. To remove unauthorized system accounts, use the following command: $ sudo userdel unauthorized_user
vulnerability.recommendation: Review and remediate: Only Authorized Local User Accounts Exist on Operating System

triage.reason: Removing unauthorized local user accounts is a standard STIG hardening step that does not affect system services or critical functionality, and is unlikely to cause lockout if performed carefully.
triage.estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Accidental removal of legitimate user accounts; Potential loss of SSH/console access if a critical account is deleted

vulnerability.title: Ensure All User Initialization Files Have Mode 0740 Or Less Permissive
vulnerability.description: Set the mode of the user initialization files, including the root user, to 0740 with the following commands: $ sudo chmod 0740 /root/.INIT_FILE $ sudo chmod 0740 /home/USER/.INIT_FILE
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: var_user_initialization_files_regex='' readarray -t interactive_users < <(awk -F: '$3==0 || $3>=1000 {print $1}' /etc/passwd) readarray -t interactive_users_home < <(awk -F: '$3==0 || $3>=1000 {print $6}' /etc/passwd) readarray -t interactive_users_shell < <(awk -F: '$3==0 || $3>=1000 {print $7}' /etc/passwd) USERS_IGNORED_REGEX='nobody|nfsnobody' for (( i=0; i<"${#interactive_users[@]}"; i++ )); do if ! grep -qP "$USERS_IGNORED_REGEX" <<< "${interactive_users[$i]}" && \ [ "${interactive_users_shell[$i]}" != "/sbin/nologin" ]; then readarray -t init_files < <(find "${interactive_users_home[$i]}" -maxdepth 1 \ -exec basename {} \; | grep -P "$var_user_initialization_files_regex") for file in "${init_files[@]}"; do chmod u-s,g-wxs,o= "${interactive_users_home[$i]}/$file" done fi done

triage.reason: Changing file permissions to 0740 for user initialization files is a standard STIG hardening action that does not affect authentication, networking, or critical services. It simply restricts access to the files, reducing the risk of unauthorized modification.

vulnerability.title: Ensure the Default Umask is Set Correctly in /etc/profile
vulnerability.description: To ensure the default umask controlled by /etc/profile is set properly, add or correct the umask setting in /etc/profile to read as follows: umask Note that /etc/profile also reads scripts within /etc/profile.d directory. These scripts are also valid files to set umask value. Therefore, they should also be considered during the check and properly remediated, if necessary.
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q kernel-core; then var_accounts_user_umask='' readarray -t profile_files < <(find /etc/profile.d/ -type f -name '*.sh' -or -name 'sh.local') for file in "${profile_files[@]}" /etc/profile; do grep -qE '^[^#]*umask' "$file" && sed -i -E "s/^(\s*umask\s*)[0-7]+/\1$var_accounts_user_umask/g" "$file" done if ! grep -qrE '^[^#]*umask' /etc/profile*; then echo "umask $var_accounts_user_umask" >> /etc/profile fi else >&2 echo 'Remediation is not applicable, nothing was done' fi

triage.reason: Setting the default umask in /etc/profile and /etc/profile.d scripts is a standard configuration change that does not affect authentication, networking, or critical services, and is unlikely to cause lockout or system breakage.
triage.estimated_impact: Incorrect umask value may lead to overly permissive file creation permissions

execution_details.stdout:
# /etc/profile

# System wide environment and startup programs, for login setup
# Functions and aliases go in /etc/bashrc

# It's NOT a good idea to change this file unless you know what you
# are doing. It's much better to create a custom.sh shell script in
# /etc/profile.d/ to make custom changes to your environment, as this
# will prevent the need for merging in future updates.

pathmunge () {
    case ":${PATH}:" in
        *:"$1":*)
            ;;
        *)
            if [ "$2" = "after" ] ; then
                PATH=$PATH:$1
            else
                PATH=$1:$PATH
            fi
    esac
}


if [ -x /usr/bin/id ]; then
    if [ -z "$EUID" ]; then
        # ksh workaround
        EUID=`/usr/bin/id -u`
        UID=`/usr/bin/id -ru`
    fi
    USER="`/usr/bin/id -un`"
    LOGNAME=$USER
    MAIL="/var/spool/mail/$USER"
fi

# Path manipulation
if [ "$EUID" = "0" ]; then
    pathmunge /usr/sbin
    pathmunge /usr/local/sbin
else
    pathmunge /usr/local/sbin after
    pathmunge /usr/sbin after
fi

HOSTNAME=$(/usr/bin/hostnamectl --transient 2>/dev/null) || \
HOSTNAME=$(/usr/bin/hostname 2>/dev/null) || \
HOSTNAME=$(/usr/bin/uname -n)

HISTSIZE=1000
if [ "$HISTCONTROL" = "ignorespace" ] ; then
    export HISTCONTROL=ignoreboth
else
    export HISTCONTROL=ignoredups
fi

export PATH USER LOGNAME MAIL HOSTNAME HISTSIZE HISTCONTROL

for i in /etc/profile.d/*.sh /etc/profile.d/sh.local ; do
    if [ -r "$i" ]; then
        if [ "${-#*i}" != "$-" ]; then 
            . "$i"
        else
            . "$i" >/dev/null
        fi
    fi
done

unset i
unset -f pathmunge

if [ -n "${BASH_VERSION-}" ] ; then
        if [ -f /etc/bashrc ] ; then
                # Bash login shells run only /etc/profile
                # Bash non-login shells run only /etc/bashrc
                # Check for double sourcing is done in /etc/bashrc.
                . /etc/bashrc
       fi
fi
umask 022

The default umask has been set to **022** system‑wide, satisfying the OpenSCAP finding.  

Key updates:

- `/etc/profile` now ends with a line `umask 022`.  
- Existing `/etc/profile.d/01-umask.sh` and `/etc/profile.d/umask.sh` already contain the correct setting and remain unchanged.

review_verdict.feedback: The plan will correctly set a default umask of 022 system‑wide and is idempotent. It meets the remediation requirement, though there is room to polish the implementation details for robustness and auditability.
concerns: Possible insertion of double spaces before the numeric value in the umask line.
Updates all occurrences of any three‑digit number after ‘umask’ regardless of context, which could inadvertently modify non‑standard lines.
If a profile.d script already contains a correct `umask 022`, the plan will still rewrite it, potentially losing existing script metadata.
The plan creates or overwrites `/etc/profile.d/umask.sh` without explicitly verifying that no user‑defined script is already handling umask logic.
No explicit audit trail is kept for each file modification.
suggested_improvements: Refine the replacement regex to preserve original indentation and to match only a three‑digit numeric value, e.g.`*\s*umask\s+[0-9]{3}*`.
Check whether a `/etc/profile.d/umask.sh` or any other script already contains a valid `umask 022` before creating or rewriting it.
Explicitly set ownership and permissions (root:root, 0644) for any new script using `chown` and `chmod`.
Add logging (e.g., write to `/var/log/umask‑remediation.log`) each time a file is modified for audit purposes.
Verify that `/etc/profile` sources `/etc/profile.d/*.sh`; if not, add a source statement or explanatory comment.

qa_result.verdict_reason: The plan correctly ensures a system-wide default umask of 022 by editing /etc/profile and profile.d scripts in a straightforward, idempotent manner. It does not alter critical service configurations, and setting umask to 022 is a standard safe default that will not break sshd, auditd, firewalld, or networking. While minor formatting changes in existing scripts are possible, these do not affect functionality, and creating a dedicated umask.sh is safe if no other script already handles umask.
side_effects: Possible alteration of comment formatting or whitespace in existing profile scripts.
Creation of /etc/profile.d/umask.sh if none existed previously, which may overwrite a user-defined file with the same name.
Slight performance overhead on shell startup due to expanded line parsing (negligible).

vulnerability.title: Set the Boot Loader Admin Username to a Non-Default Value
vulnerability.description: The grub2 boot loader should have a superuser account and password protection enabled to protect boot-time settings. To maximize the protection, select a password-protected superuser account with unique name, and modify the /etc/grub.d/01_users configuration file to reflect the account name change. Do not to use common administrator account names like root, admin, or administrator for the grub2 superuser account. Change the superuser to a different username (The default is 'root'). $ sed -i 's/\(set superusers=\).*/\1"<unique user ID>"/g' /etc/grub.d/01_users The line mentioned above must be followed by the line export superusers so that the superusers is honored. Once the superuser account has been added, update the grub.cfg file by running: grub2-mkconfig -o /boot/grub2/grub.cfg
vulnerability.recommendation: Review and remediate: Set the Boot Loader Admin Username to a Non-Default Value

triage.reason: Changing the grub2 superuser username is a bootloader configuration change that can lock out boot-time access if misconfigured. According to policy, bootloader changes are classified as too dangerous to remediate without careful review.
triage.estimated_impact: reboot required; authentication/authorization affected; Bootloader misconfiguration; Loss of boot-time access; Potential system lockout

vulnerability.title: Set Boot Loader Password in grub2
vulnerability.description: The grub2 boot loader should have a superuser account and password protection enabled to protect boot-time settings. Since plaintext passwords are a security risk, generate a hash for the password by running the following command: # grub2-setpassword When prompted, enter the password that was selected.
vulnerability.recommendation: Review and remediate: Set Boot Loader Password in grub2

triage.reason: Setting a boot loader password is a bootloader change that can lock the system or prevent boot if misconfigured, making it too dangerous to remediate without careful planning.
triage.estimated_impact: reboot required; Boot failure; Loss of console access; Potential system unbootable

vulnerability.title: Configure Multiple DNS Servers in /etc/resolv.conf
vulnerability.description: Determine whether the system is using local or DNS name resolution with the following command: $ sudo grep hosts /etc/nsswitch.conf hosts: files dns If the DNS entry is missing from the host's line in the "/etc/nsswitch.conf" file, the "/etc/resolv.conf" file must be empty. Verify the "/etc/resolv.conf" file is empty with the following command: $ sudo ls -al /etc/resolv.conf -rw-r--r-- 1 root root 0 Aug 19 08:31 resolv.conf If the DNS entry is found on the host's line of the "/etc/nsswitch.conf" file, then verify the following: Multiple Domain Name System (DNS) Servers should be configured in /etc/resolv.conf. This provides redundant name resolution services in the event that a domain server crashes. To configure the system to contain as least 2 DNS servers, add a corresponding nameserver ip_address entry in /etc/resolv.conf for each DNS server where ip_address is the IP address of a valid DNS server. For example: search example.com nameserver 192.168.0.1 nameserver 192.168.0.2
vulnerability.recommendation: Review and remediate: Configure Multiple DNS Servers in /etc/resolv.conf

triage.reason: Adding multiple DNS servers to /etc/resolv.conf is a standard configuration change that does not affect authentication, authorization, or critical services, and is unlikely to cause lockout or system breakage.
triage.estimated_impact: networking affected; Incorrect DNS entries may cause name resolution failures

review_verdict.feedback: The plan will correctly add at least two DNS servers when DNS is enabled in nsswitch, addressing the vulnerability. It could be refined for robustness and flexibility.
concerns: Use of hard‑coded Google DNS servers may not be appropriate for all environments.
Basic string check for 'dns' may miss comma‑separated or weighted entries.
No validation that IP addresses are syntactically valid or non‑duplicate.
suggested_improvements: Parameterize DNS server addresses instead of hard‑coding them.
Implement regex or split parsing to robustly detect 'dns' within the hosts line.
Validate IP format and check for existing duplicate entries before appending.
Preserve any existing search lines, comments, or ordering in /etc/resolv.conf when writing.

qa_result.verdict_reason: The remediation correctly ensures at least two DNS servers are configured when DNS is enabled in /etc/nsswitch.conf. It only modifies /etc/resolv.conf, preserves existing entries, and adds a redundant resolver, which is unlikely to break core services or cause lockouts. The impact is minimal and reversible.

vulnerability.title: Disable Kernel Parameter for IPv4 Forwarding on all IPv4 Interfaces
vulnerability.description: To set the runtime status of the net.ipv4.conf.all.forwarding kernel parameter, run the following command: $ sudo sysctl -w net.ipv4.conf.all.forwarding=0 To make sure that the setting is persistent, add the following line to a file in the directory /etc/sysctl.d: net.ipv4.conf.all.forwarding = 0
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q kernel-core; then # Comment out any occurrences of net.ipv4.conf.all.forwarding from /etc/sysctl.d/*.conf files for f in /etc/sysctl.d/*.conf /run/sysctl.d/*.conf /usr/local/lib/sysctl.d/*.conf; do # skip systemd-sysctl symlink (/etc/sysctl.d/99-sysctl.conf -> /etc/sysctl.conf) if [[ "$(readlink -f "$f")" == "/etc/sysctl.conf" ]]; then continue; fi matching_list=$(grep -P '^(?!#).*[\s]*net.ipv4.conf.all.forwarding.*$' $f | uniq ) if ! test -z "$matching_list"; then while IFS= read -r entry; do escaped_entry=$(sed -e 's|/|\\/|g' <<< "$entry") # comment out "net.ipv4.conf.all.forwarding" matches to preserve user data sed -i --follow-symlinks "s/^${escaped_entry}$/# &/g" $f done <<< "$matching_list" fi done # # Set sysctl

triage.reason: Disabling IPv4 forwarding is a standard sysctl hardening measure that does not affect authentication, authorization, or critical services. The change is local to the kernel networking stack and can be applied safely without risk of lockout.
triage.estimated_impact: networking affected

review_verdict.feedback: The plan will correctly set the IPv4 forwarding sysctl to 0 at runtime and persist the setting in /etc/sysctl.d/, thus fully addressing the vulnerability.
concerns: The plan only processes files under /etc/sysctl.d/*.conf and does not explicitly check /etc/sysctl.conf, /run/sysctl.d/*.conf, or /usr/local/lib/sysctl.d/*.conf. If a higher priority file in those directories sets the parameter to 1, the change may be overridden.
The sed usage may comment out lines with leading whitespace but could miss lines that contain the parameter followed by comments or other parameters on the same line.
suggested_improvements: Include checks for /etc/sysctl.conf and the /run/sysctl.d and /usr/local/lib/sysctl.d directories when searching for existing non‑commented occurrences.
Add an explicit sysctl reload step (e.g., `sysctl --system` or `systemctl restart systemd-sysctl`) after the persistence change to ensure changes take effect on all running kernel interfaces.
Consider creating a single, well‑documented config file (e.g., 90-ssg-ipv4-forwarding.conf) and removing or commenting out any other conflicting settings rather than modifying every file.
Use a more robust search pattern such as `grep -E '^ net.ipv4.conf.all.forwarding '` to match the key accurately and preserve comments.
Add file ownership and permissions checks (e.g., owner root and mode 644) after creating or editing the config file.

qa_result.verdict_reason: The remediation sets the kernel parameter net.ipv4.conf.all.forwarding to 0 at runtime and ensures persistence via a high‑priority /etc/sysctl.d/99‑ssg‑ipv4‑forwarding.conf file, which overrides any earlier settings. This direct, minimal change is standard for hardening IPv4 forwarding security and should not disrupt critical services such as sshd, auditd, or firewalld in normal host mode. No significant side effects are expected beyond the intended disabling of IP forwarding, a benign impact when the host is not a router or VPN server.
side_effects: May prevent services that rely on IP forwarding (e.g., VPNs, NAT/k8s routing) from functioning correctly if the host already performs forwarding.

vulnerability.title: Ensure All World-Writable Directories Are Owned by root User
vulnerability.description: All directories in local partitions which are world-writable should be owned by root. If any world-writable directories are not owned by root, this should be investigated. Following this, the files should be deleted or assigned to root user.
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # At least under containerized env /proc can have files w/o possilibity to # modify even as root. And touching /proc is not good idea anyways. find / -path /proc -prune -o \ -not -fstype afs -not -fstype autofs -not -fstype ceph -not -fstype cifs -not -fstype smb3 \ -not -fstype smbfs -not -fstype sshfs -not -fstype ncpfs -not -fstype ncp -not -fstype nfs \ -not -fstype nfs4 -not -fstype gfs -not -fstype gfs2 -not -fstype glusterfs -not -fstype gpfs \ -not -fstype pvfs2 -not -fstype ocfs2 -not -fstype lustre -not -fstype davfs \ -not -fstype fuse.sshfs -type d -perm -0002 -uid +0 -exec chown root {} \;

triage.reason: Changing ownership of world-writable directories to root is a standard STIG hardening practice that does not affect authentication, networking, or critical services. The operation is limited to file permissions and is unlikely to cause lockout or system breakage.
triage.estimated_impact: filesystems affected; Potential disruption if an application relies on non-root ownership of a world-writable directory

vulnerability.title: Verify that All World-Writable Directories Have Sticky Bits Set
vulnerability.description: When the so-called 'sticky bit' is set on a directory, only the owner of a given file may remove that file from the directory. Without the sticky bit, any user with write access to a directory may remove any file in the directory. Setting the sticky bit prevents users from removing each other's files. In cases where there is no reason for a directory to be world-writable, a better solution is to remove that permission rather than to set the sticky bit. However, if a directory is used by a particular application, consult that application's documentation instead of blindly changing modes. To set the sticky bit on a world-writable directory DIR, run the following command: $ sudo chmod +t DIR
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: df --local -P | awk '{if (NR!=1) print $6}' \ | xargs -I '$6' find '$6' -xdev -type d \ \( -perm -0002 -a ! -perm -1000 \) 2>/dev/null \ -exec chmod a+t {} +

triage.reason: Setting the sticky bit on world-writable directories is a standard file permission hardening measure that does not affect authentication, networking, or critical services. It prevents users from deleting each other's files without causing lockout or system breakage.
triage.estimated_impact: filesystems affected; World-writable directories may allow unintended file deletion

vulnerability.description: To properly set the permissions of /etc/gshadow-, run the command: $ sudo chmod 0000 /etc/gshadow-
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: chmod u-xwrs,g-xwrs,o-xwrt /etc/gshadow-

triage.estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; filesystems affected

llm_verdict.message: The permissions on `/etc/passwd` have been updated to `0644`. This remediation satisfies the OpenSCAP finding.

review_verdict.feedback: The plan correctly addresses the vulnerability by applying the recommended permissions to /etc/passwd. It is sufficient to run chmod 0644 on the file and does not introduce new risks.
suggested_improvements: Add a verification step after chmod to ensure the file permissions are 0644 and the ownership remains root:root.
Consider adding a catch for potential SELinux or AppArmor contexts that could be affected, though typically not relevant for /etc/passwd.

qa_result.verdict_reason: Setting /etc/passwd to 0644 is the standard permission for the file and does not interfere with SSH, PAM, or any other service that requires reading user account information. The plan applies the exact recommended permissions and is unlikely to lock out authentication or crash system services.

vulnerability.title: Verify Permissions on shadow File
vulnerability.description: To properly set the permissions of /etc/shadow, run the command: $ sudo chmod 0000 /etc/shadow
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: chmod u-xwrs,g-xwrs,o-xwrt /etc/shadow

triage.reason: Setting /etc/shadow permissions to 0000 is a standard STIG hardening step that does not risk service disruption or lockout.
triage.estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected

remediation.scan_output: Vulnerability file_permissions_etc_shadow not found in single-rule scan (possibly fixed)

llm_verdict.message: The permissions on **/etc/shadow** have been successfully set to `0000`, aligning with the required security policy.

all_attempts.scan_output: Vulnerability file_permissions_etc_shadow not found in single-rule scan (possibly fixed)

llm_verdict.message: The permissions on **/etc/shadow** have been successfully set to `0000`, aligning with the required security policy.

review_verdict.feedback: The plan correctly addresses the permission issue by setting /etc/shadow to mode 0000, which aligns with the STIG recommendation and resolves the vulnerability.
concerns: Does not verify or maintain the correct ownership (root:root) or SELinux context, which could inadvertently change after the chmod. 0000 does not create any permission issues for root access, but if the file’s ownership were incorrect, other services might fail. The plan does not handle potential audit or logging of the change.
Using 'chmod u-xwrs,g-xwrs,o-xwrt' is equivalent to 'chmod 0000', but the verbose form may be unnecessarily complex. The plan assumes the command will run successfully without error.
suggested_improvements: Confirm that /etc/shadow is owned by root:root before and after the chmod. Use 'chown root:root /etc/shadow' if necessary.
Check and preserve SELinux context (e.g., 'restorecon -v /etc/shadow') after changing permissions to avoid disrupting SELinux.
Use the simpler 'chmod 0000 /etc/shadow' if only permissions need to be set, unless a more descriptive command is preferred.
Add audit logging or check for any errors from the chmod command before proceeding to next steps.
Document the change for future maintenance and verify the effect with 'ls -lZ /etc/shadow' if applicable.

qa_result.verdict_reason: Setting /etc/shadow to 0000 is the standard and correct remediation for this vulnerability; root retains read access and all PAM, sshd, and login services are designed to read the file with root privileges. The chmod command does not alter ownership or SELinux context, and no critical services will be disrupted.

vulnerability.title: Verify Group Who Owns /var/log Directory
vulnerability.description: To properly set the group owner of /var/log, run the command: $ sudo chgrp root /var/log
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: newgroup="" if getent group "0" >/dev/null 2>&1; then newgroup="0" fi if [[ -z "${newgroup}" ]]; then >&2 echo "0 is not a defined group on the system" else find -P /var/log/ -maxdepth 0 -type d ! -group 0 -exec chgrp --no-dereference "$newgroup" {} \; fi

triage.reason: Changing the group owner of /var/log to root is a standard file permission adjustment that does not affect authentication, networking, or critical services. It follows typical STIG hardening patterns and is unlikely to cause lockout or system breakage.
triage.estimated_impact: filesystems affected; None

vulnerability.title: Add nosuid Option to /boot/efi
vulnerability.description: The nosuid mount option can be used to prevent execution of setuid programs in /boot/efi. The SUID and SGID permissions should not be required on the boot partition. Add the nosuid option to the fourth column of /etc/fstab for the line which controls mounting of /boot/efi.

( [ -f /.dockerenv ] || [ -f /run/.containerenv ] ) ) && { findmnt --kernel "/boot/efi" > /dev/null || findmnt --fstab "/boot/efi" > /dev/null; }; then function perform_remediation { # the mount point /boot/efi has to be defined in /etc/fstab # before this remediation can be executed. In case it is not defined, the # remediation aborts and no changes regarding the mount point are done. mount_point_match_regexp="$(printf "^[[:space:]]*[^#].*[[:space:]]%s[[:space:]]" "/boot/efi")" grep "$mount_point_match_regexp" -q /etc/fstab \ || { echo

triage.reason: Adding the nosuid mount option to /boot/efi is a standard hardening step that does not affect authentication, networking, or critical services. It only prevents execution of setuid programs on the boot partition, which is a low-risk change.
triage.estimated_impact: filesystems affected; None

review_verdict.feedback: The proposed sed command incorrectly appends "nosuid" to the filesystem type (the first non‑space field after the mount point) instead of the options field, which will corrupt /etc/fstab and likely prevent proper mounting of /boot/efi. As written, the plan would not resolve the vulnerability and could render the system unbootable.
concerns: sed command modifies the wrong column, risking fstab corruption
no backup of the original /etc/fstab before modification
no check to ensure nosuid is absent or present before performing replacement
no validation that the fstab entry follows expected whitespace formatting
suggested_improvements: Use a more precise pattern to target the fourth field (mount options) in /etc/fstab, e.g., `sed -E -i 's/^([[:space:]]*\S+[[:space:]]+\S+[[:space:]]+\S+)([[:space:]]+[^,]+)([[:space:]]+.*)$/\1\2,nosuid\3/' /etc/fstab`
Take a backup of /etc/fstab before editing or use `cp /etc/fstab /etc/fstab.bak`
Confirm presence of /boot/efi entry and check for existing nosuid before applying changes
Consider using `awk` or a small script to append nosuid only if missing, preserving existing options

pre_approval.rejection_reason: The proposed sed command incorrectly appends "nosuid" to the filesystem type (the first non‑space field after the mount point) instead of the options field, which will corrupt /etc/fstab and likely prevent proper mounting of /boot/efi. As written, the plan would not resolve the vulnerability and could render the system unbootable.

commands_executed:
python3 - <<'PY'
import pathlib, re
f=pathlib.Path('/etc/fstab')
lines=f.read_text().splitlines()
out=[]
for line in lines:
    if not line.strip() or line.lstrip().startswith('#'):
        out.append(line)
        continue
    parts=re.split(r'\s+', line, maxsplit=5)
    if len(parts)<6:
        out.append(line)
        continue
    if parts[1]=='/boot':
        opts=parts[3]
        if 'nodev' not in opts.split(','):
            if opts=='defaults':
                opts='defaults,nodev'
            else:
                opts=opts+','+'nodev'
            parts[3]=opts
    out.append(' '.join(parts))
f.write_text('\n'.join(out)+'\n')
PY

python3 - <<'PY'
import pathlib, re
fstab=pathlib.Path('/etc/fstab')
lines=fstab.read_text().splitlines()
new_lines=[]
for line in lines:
    stripped=line.strip()
    if not stripped or stripped.startswith('#'):
        new_lines.append(line)
        continue
    # split by spaces/tabs but keep the original field positions
    parts=re.split(r'\s+', line, maxsplit=5)
    if len(parts)<6:
        new_lines.append(line)
        continue
    if parts[1] == '/boot':
        opts=parts[3]
        # ensure nodev present
        opts_set=set(opts.split(','))
        if 'nodev' not in opts_set:
            opts_set.add('nodev')
            parts[3]=','.join(sorted(opts_set))
    new_lines.append(' '.join(parts))
print('\n'.join(new_lines))
fstab.write_text('\n'.join(new_lines)+'\n')
PY

import pathlib, re

backup = pathlib.Path('/etc/fstab.bak')
fstab = pathlib.Path('/etc/fstab')

data = backup.read_text().splitlines()
new = []
for line in data:
    stripped = line.strip()
    if not stripped or stripped.startswith('#'):
        new.append(line)
        continue
    parts = re.split(r'\s+', line, maxsplit=5)
    if len(parts) < 6:
        new.append(line)
        continue
    if parts[1] == '/boot':
        opts = parts[3]
        opts_set = set(filter(None, opts.split(',')))
        opts_set.add('nodev')
        parts[3] = ','.join(sorted(opts_set))
    new.append(' '.join(parts))

fstab.write_text('\n'.join(new)+'\n')
print('Updated fstab')
PY

python3 - <<'PY'
import re
import pathlib

backup=pathlib.Path('/etc/fstab.bak')
content=backup.read_text().splitlines()
new=[]
for line in content:
    if re.search(r'\s+/boot\s+', line) and not line.lstrip().startswith('#'):
        # modify
        parts=re.split(r'\s+', line, maxsplit=5)
        # parts like [device, mount, filetyp, options, dump, pass]
        if len(parts)>=6:
            opts=parts[3]
            if 'nodev' not in opts.split(','):
                opts_set=set(opts.split(','))
                opts_set.add('nodev')
                parts[3]=','.join(sorted(opts_set))
        new_line=' '.join(parts)
        new.append(new_line)
        continue
    new.append(line)

new_file='\n'.join(new)+'\n'
with open('/etc/fstab', 'w') as f:
    f.write(new_file)
print('Updated fstab')
PY

execution_details.command:
python3 - <<'PY'
import pathlib, re
fstab=pathlib.Path('/etc/fstab')
lines=fstab.read_text().splitlines()
new_lines=[]
for line in lines:
    stripped=line.strip()
    if not stripped or stripped.startswith('#'):
        new_lines.append(line)
        continue
    # split by spaces/tabs but keep the original field positions
    parts=re.split(r'\s+', line, maxsplit=5)
    if len(parts)<6:
        new_lines.append(line)
        continue
    if parts[1] == '/boot':
        opts=parts[3]
        # ensure nodev present
        opts_set=set(opts.split(','))
        if 'nodev' not in opts_set:
            opts_set.add('nodev')
            parts[3]=','.join(sorted(opts_set))
    new_lines.append(' '.join(parts))
print('\n'.join(new_lines))
fstab.write_text('\n'.join(new_lines)+'\n')
PY

import pathlib, re

backup = pathlib.Path('/etc/fstab.bak')
fstab = pathlib.Path('/etc/fstab')

data = backup.read_text().splitlines()
new = []
for line in data:
    stripped = line.strip()
    if not stripped or stripped.startswith('#'):
        new.append(line)
        continue
    parts = re.split(r'\s+', line, maxsplit=5)
    if len(parts) < 6:
        new.append(line)
        continue
    if parts[1] == '/boot':
        opts = parts[3]
        opts_set = set(filter(None, opts.split(',')))
        opts_set.add('nodev')
        parts[3] = ','.join(sorted(opts_set))
    new.append(' '.join(parts))

fstab.write_text('\n'.join(new)+'\n')
print('Updated fstab')
PY

python3 - <<'PY'
import re
import pathlib

backup=pathlib.Path('/etc/fstab.bak')
content=backup.read_text().splitlines()
new=[]
for line in content:
    if re.search(r'\s+/boot\s+', line) and not line.lstrip().startswith('#'):
        # modify
        parts=re.split(r'\s+', line, maxsplit=5)
        # parts like [device, mount, filetyp, options, dump, pass]
        if len(parts)>=6:
            opts=parts[3]
            if 'nodev' not in opts.split(','):
                opts_set=set(opts.split(','))
                opts_set.add('nodev')
                parts[3]=','.join(sorted(opts_set))
        new_line=' '.join(parts)
        new.append(new_line)
        continue
    new.append(line)

new_file='\n'.join(new)+'\n'
with open('/etc/fstab', 'w') as f:
    f.write(new_file)
print('Updated fstab')
PY

        continue

            else:

    out.append(' '.join(parts))

review_verdict.feedback: The plan will correctly add the nodev option to /boot in /etc/fstab and remount the partition.  It does not introduce new risks and will resolve the vulnerability as defined.
concerns: The sed pattern used may accidentally match unintended lines if the fstab file contains unusual spacing or comments.  Any inline comments on the same line could be removed if not handled carefully.
The plan does not back up /etc/fstab before modification, so accidental corruption would be hard to recover from without manual intervention.
The remount command runs unconditionally; if the fstab edit fails or /boot is not present, remounting may fail or result in a partial state.
suggested_improvements: Use a robust backup of /etc/fstab before editing (e.g., cp /etc/fstab /etc/fstab.bak).

review_verdict.feedback: The plan correctly adds missing nosuid, performs verification and rollback, and preserves other fstab fields. It addresses the issues raised in prior reviews and will resolve the vulnerability when executed.
concerns: The script assumes /boot is a separate mountpoint – if it is not, nosuid will have no effect and the script will exit silently.
It does not explicitly warn if /boot is part of the root filesystem, which may be noteworthy for hardening discussions.
The line matching uses exact string '/boot'; entries like 'LABEL=/boot' or '/dev/sda1 /boot' with missing device field might bypass the check.
Trailing comments are not explicitly preserved; although fstab does not support inline comments, any stray whitespace may be lost across the rewrite.
suggested_improvements: Add a preliminary check to confirm /boot is mounted on its own filesystem, or warn the operator if it is part of the root mount.
Enhance the pattern to accommodate LABEL or UUID styles, e.g., look for lines where the second field ends with '/boot' or contains '/boot' after any leading path.
After remounting, explicitly confirm the mount options string includes 'nosuid' using `findmnt -n -o OPTIONS /boot` for a more reliable check than parsing `mount` output.
Document the backup filename convention to include a description of change, such as `fstab.bak-<timestamp>-nosuid` for easier audit.

qa_result.verdict_reason: The remediation plan correctly adds the nosuid option to the /boot entry in /etc/fstab, creates a backup, applies the change atomically, and verifies the mount. It does not interfere with critical services such as sshd, auditd, firewalld, or networking. The only minor risk is that if /boot is not a separate mount point, the remount command may fail, but this would not affect system operation. The plan also includes rollback if verification fails, making it safe.
side_effects: remount command may error if /boot is not a separate mount point (not a functional impact).
If /boot entry missing or misformatted, script exits quietly – acceptable.

Add the nodev option to the fourth column of /etc/fstab for the line which controls mounting of /dev/shm.

review_verdict.feedback: The plan correctly covers the essential remediation: adding the nodev option to /etc/fstab for /dev/shm and remounting the filesystem. The script is idempotent and only changes the relevant mount line, minimizing impact. However, the sed pattern and handling of commented or missing entries could be fragile, and a pre‑check for existing nodev is needed to avoid duplication. Overall the plan will resolve the vulnerability when executed as intended.
concerns: SED expression may mis‑parse lines with irregular spacing or leading tabs, potentially leaving the nodev option absent or duplicated.
Commented /dev/shm entries are not modified – if the line is commented, the plan will leave it unchanged.
The plan does not explicitly create the mount line if it is missing entirely.
No explicit handling of the 'nodev' presence check prior to sed execution is shown, which could result in duplicate entries if not properly implemented.

llm_verdict.message:
**Remediation Completed**  

1. Backed up `/etc/fstab`.  
2. Added `nosuid` to the `/dev/shm` entry.  
3. Remounted `/dev/shm` to apply the change.  

The system now satisfies the OpenSCAP finding.

review_verdict.feedback: Based on the stated plan, the proposed actions would successfully add the nosuid option to the /dev/shm entry in /etc/fstab and thereby satisfy the remediation requirement. The approach is straightforward and targets only the relevant line, preserving other options. However, there are a few areas that could be tightened and a missing step that is necessary for a fully functional fix.
concerns: 1) The provided sed expression is overly complex and may not correctly match lines that have no fourth column or where the options list contains spaces or commas. 2) The plan only modifies the fstab file; it does not remount /dev/shm on the running system, so the change will only take effect after a reboot or a manual remount. 3) The script does not provide a fallback or warning if the /dev/shm entry is not present in fstab (e.g., it might be mounted by systemd via tmpfiles).
4) No error handling is described; failed sed edits would leave fstab corrupted.
5) No backup of the original fstab is made before editing, which could complicate rollback if the edit fails.
suggested_improvements:
1) Simplify the sed command to reliably append the option: 
   ```sudo sed -i '/^\s*[^#]*\/dev\/shm[[:space:]]/ { s|\(\s*[^#]*[[:space:]]\+/dev/shm[[:space:]]\)\(.*\)|\1\2,nosuid|' }' /etc/fstab```
2) Add a backup step: `cp /etc/fstab /etc/fstab.bak` before editing.
3) After editing, remount the filesystem: `mount -o remount /dev/shm` or advise a reboot.
4) Verify that the entry exists; if not, create a line with `tmpfs  /dev/shm  tmpfs defaults,nosuid  0  0`.
5) Include basic error checks (e.g., exit if sed fails).
6) Log the change to an audit file for traceability.
7) Optionally use `systemd-tmpfiles --create` if systemd manages /dev/shm.

qa_result.verdict_reason: The plan updates /etc/fstab to add the nosuid option for /dev/shm, which directly addresses the vulnerability without affecting critical runtime services. The edit is targeted, does not alter other options, and has minimal risk of breaking services like sshd or auditd.
side_effects: Potential fstab corruption if the sed expression fails; recommend making a backup of /etc/fstab before editing.

review_verdict.feedback: The updated script now preserves whitespace, creates a reliable temporary backup, remounts /home for immediate effect, and validates the fstab syntax, so the vulnerability will be resolved without harmful side effects.
concerns: If multiple /home entries exist, only the first matched line will be updated; no deduplication of existing noexec option; possible edge cases with bind mounts or subvolumes are not explicitly handled.
suggested_improvements: Add a loop to process all /home entries or explicitly warn if more than one exists; check if noexec already present before appending; handle bind mounts by preserving bind flag if present; add a final check to ensure `mount | grep '/home'` shows the option after remount.

qa_result.verdict_reason: The script correctly identifies the /home entry in /etc/fstab, backs up the file, preserves whitespace, adds the noexec option (or updates it if already present), validates the fstab syntax, and remounts /home immediately. It does not alter the mount point or other options, and the remount is safe even when active user sessions exist. The only functional change is that binaries and scripts located directly in /home can no longer be executed via the kernel; this is expected and does not affect critical services such as sshd, auditd, or firewalld. Therefore, the remediation is likely safe.
side_effects: Users cannot execute binaries/scripts located directly in /home; startup scripts with shebang may fail if run as executables.
Remounting /home may briefly interrupt any long‐running processes that are writing to /home during the operation.
If /etc/fstab contains multiple /home entries (e.g., bind mounts or subvolumes), only the first match is modified, potentially leaving other entries without noexec.
services_affected: sshd (login shells may fail to execute scripts in /home if used as executables)
user login shells (cannot run binaries in home)

llm_verdict.message:
**PLAN**

1. **Verify /home entry in `/etc/fstab`**  
   * Read the file to ensure there is a non‑comment line that mounts `/home`.*

2. **Check whether the `nosuid` option is already present on that line**  
   * If the line contains `nosuid` in the options column, no changes are needed.  
   * If `nosuid` is missing, proceed to step 3.

3. **Add the `nosuid` option**  
   * Use `sed -i` to append `nosuid` to the options column of the `/home` line only.  
   * Pattern:  
     ```bash
     sed -i 's#^\(\S\+\s\+/home\s\+\S\+\s\+\)\([^ \t]*\)#\1\2,nosuid#' /etc/fstab
     ```  
     * This regex matches the line that mounts `/home` (device, mount‑point, filesystem) and captures the existing options in `\2`.  
     * It then replaces `\2` with `\2,nosuid`, preserving whatever other options are present.

4. **No other changes**  
   * Do not edit commented lines or other mount points.

5. **Sequence of tool calls**  
   * `read_file` to get `/etc/fstab` for inspection.  
   * Optional checks (grep) to decide if the sed command should run.  
   * `run_cmd` with the sed command above.

**RATIONALE**  
Adding `nosuid` to the `/home` mount option prevents execution of set‑uid binaries in user directories, a standard hardening step. The operation is reversible (edit the file back) and does not affect critical services or authentication. This change complements the other system configurations and stays within the minimal‑impact remediation policy.

---

Also, the plan does not remount /home after the change or plan for reboot, so the change will take effect only after a remount or reboot.

vulnerability.title: Add nodev Option to Non-Root Local Partitions
vulnerability.description: The nodev mount option prevents files from being interpreted as character or block devices. Legitimate character and block devices should exist only in the /dev directory on the root partition or within chroot jails built for system services. Add the nodev option to the fourth column of /etc/fstab for the line which controls mounting of any non-root local partitions.
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if ( ! ( { rpm --quiet -q kernel-core ;} && { rpm --quiet -q rpm-ostree ;} && { rpm --quiet -q bootc ;} && { ! rpm --quiet -q openshift-kubelet ;} && ([ -f /run/ostree-booted ] || [ -L /ostree ]) ) && ! ( [ -f /.dockerenv ] || [ -f /run/.containerenv ] ) ); then MOUNT_OPTION="nodev" # Create array of local non-root partitions readarray -t partitions_records < <(findmnt --mtab --raw --evaluate | grep "^/\w" | grep -v "^/proc" | grep "\s/dev/\w") # Create array of polyinstantiated directories, in case one of them is found in mtab readarray -t polyinstantiated_dirs < \ <(grep -oP "^\s*[^#\s]+\s+\S+" /etc/security/namespace.conf | grep -oP "(?<=\s)\S+?(?=/?\$)") # Define excluded non-local file systems excluded_fstypes=( afs autofs ceph cif

triage.reason: Adding the nodev mount option to non‑root local partitions is a standard hardening practice that prevents files from being interpreted as character or block devices. It does not affect system operation or access and is unlikely to cause lockout.
triage.estimated_impact: filesystems affected; Potential impact on applications that rely on device files in non‑root partitions

vulnerability.description: To set the runtime status of the user.max_user_namespaces kernel parameter, run the following command: $ sudo sysctl -w user.max_user_namespaces=0 To make sure that the setting is persistent, add the following line to a file in the directory /etc/sysctl.d: user.max_user_namespaces = 0 When containers are deployed on the machine, the value should be set to large non-zero value.

vulnerability.title: Restrict Exposed Kernel Pointer Addresses Access
vulnerability.description: To set the runtime status of the kernel.kptr_restrict kernel parameter, run the following command: $ sudo sysctl -w kernel.kptr_restrict= To make sure that the setting is persistent, add the following line to a file in the directory /etc/sysctl.d: kernel.kptr_restrict =
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q kernel-core; then # Comment out any occurrences of kernel.kptr_restrict from /etc/sysctl.d/*.conf files for f in /etc/sysctl.d/*.conf /run/sysctl.d/*.conf /usr/local/lib/sysctl.d/*.conf; do # skip systemd-sysctl symlink (/etc/sysctl.d/99-sysctl.conf -> /etc/sysctl.conf) if [[ "$(readlink -f "$f")" == "/etc/sysctl.conf" ]]; then continue; fi matching_list=$(grep -P '^(?!#).*[\s]*kernel.kptr_restrict.*$' $f | uniq ) if ! test -z "$matching_list"; then while IFS= read -r entry; do escaped_entry=$(sed -e 's|/|\\/|g' <<< "$entry") # comment out "kernel.kptr_restrict" matches to preserve user data sed -i --follow-symlinks "s/^${escaped_entry}$/# &/g" $f done <<< "$matching_list" fi done # # Set sysctl config file which to sa

triage.reason: Changing kernel.kptr_restrict is a standard sysctl configuration that does not affect authentication, networking, or filesystem integrity and is unlikely to cause lockout or system breakage.
triage.estimated_impact: Exposure of kernel pointer addresses

The kernel.kptr_restrict setting has been configured and applied successfully.

**File added/updated**  
`/etc/sysctl.d/99-kptr.conf`  
```
kernel.kptr_restrict = 1
```

**Runtime setting applied**  
`sysctl -w kernel.kptr_restrict=1` (current value: `1`)

All changes are in place and compliant with the OpenSCAP finding.
