review_verdict.feedback: The plan does not address existing kernel.kptr_restrict settings in other sysctl configuration files (e.g., /run/sysctl.d/*.conf, /etc/sysctl.d/*.conf). If any of those files set a different value, the resulting persistent configuration may not enforce the desired setting. This could lead to the vulnerability remaining unremediated after reboot. Additionally, the plan only adds a line to /etc/sysctl.conf and a new file, which may create duplicate settings or leave earlier values active. The runtime update via sysctl --system is correct, but the absence of cleanup or commenting out of previous entries is a significant omission.
concerns: Non‑empty other sysctl configuration files may still set kernel.kptr_restrict to a different value.
Potential duplicate entries if multiple lines exist.
Installation may not be idempotent if other files contain the parameter.
The plan does not perform a pre‑check of the running kernel value before writing configuration.
Missing real‑time sysctl -w command for immediate effect (though sysctl --system suffices after change).
suggested_improvements: Search and comment out or remove all other occurrences of kernel.kptr_restrict in /etc/sysctl.d/*.conf, /run/sysctl.d/*.conf, /usr/local/lib/sysctl.d/*.conf before adding your line.
Add idempotent checks to avoid duplicate lines when editing files.
Confirm current runtime value with sysctl kernel.kptr_restrict and skip write if already 1.
Use sysctl -w kernel.kptr_restrict=1 to apply immediately if desired.
Document the order of sysctl file processing to ensure your file overrides others.

pre_approval.rejection_reason: The plan does not address existing kernel.kptr_restrict settings in other sysctl configuration files (e.g., /run/sysctl.d/*.conf, /etc/sysctl.d/*.conf). If any of those files set a different value, the resulting persistent configuration may not enforce the desired setting. This could lead to the vulnerability remaining unremediated after reboot. Additionally, the plan only adds a line to /etc/sysctl.conf and a new file, which may create duplicate settings or leave earlier values active. The runtime update via sysctl --system is correct, but the absence of cleanup or commenting out of previous entries is a significant omission.

vulnerability.title: Enable Randomized Layout of Virtual Address Space
vulnerability.description: To set the runtime status of the kernel.randomize_va_space kernel parameter, run the following command: $ sudo sysctl -w kernel.randomize_va_space=2 To make sure that the setting is persistent, add the following line to a file in the directory /etc/sysctl.d: kernel.randomize_va_space = 2
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q kernel-core; then # Comment out any occurrences of kernel.randomize_va_space from /etc/sysctl.d/*.conf files for f in /etc/sysctl.d/*.conf /run/sysctl.d/*.conf /usr/local/lib/sysctl.d/*.conf; do # skip systemd-sysctl symlink (/etc/sysctl.d/99-sysctl.conf -> /etc/sysctl.conf) if [[ "$(readlink -f "$f")" == "/etc/sysctl.conf" ]]; then continue; fi matching_list=$(grep -P '^(?!#).*[\s]*kernel.randomize_va_space.*$' $f | uniq ) if ! test -z "$matching_list"; then while IFS= read -r entry; do escaped_entry=$(sed -e 's|/|\\/|g' <<< "$entry") # comment out "kernel.randomize_va_space" matches to preserve user data sed -i --follow-symlinks "s/^${escaped_entry}$/# &/g" $f done <<< "$matching_list" fi done # # Set sysctl config f

triage.reason: Setting kernel.randomize_va_space to 2 is a standard sysctl change that enhances security without affecting authentication, networking, or filesystem operations. It does not risk lockout or service disruption.

review_verdict.feedback: The plan will effectively enforce kernel.randomize_va_space=2 but could be refined to avoid unintended side effects.
concerns: Overwriting the custom config file may remove other legitimate settings that users expect to remain.
The workflow does not automatically reload sysctl after making persistent changes, which could delay the policy taking effect immediately.
The sed pattern may comment out lines that contain the keyword as part of comments or other options, potentially altering unrelated settings.
Symlinked or system-provided config files (e.g., /etc/sysctl.conf) are not explicitly excluded, which might lead to duplicate or conflicting entries.
suggested_improvements:
Check the target config file for other non‑kernel.randomize_va_space settings and preserve them instead of wiping the file entirely; append the directive if it’s missing.
Use a more precise regex in sed to match only the exact key/value pair.
After modifying any sysctl configuration file, run `sysctl --system` or `service systemd-sysctl restart` to apply changes without reboot.
Back up configuration files before editing, or use `cp` to preserve originals.
Explicitly skip known system symlinks like /etc/sysctl.conf when scanning files.

vulnerability.title: Elevate The SELinux Context When An Administrator Calls The Sudo Command
vulnerability.description: Configure the operating system to elevate the SELinux context when an administrator calls the sudo command. Edit a file in the /etc/sudoers.d directory with the following command: sudo visudo -f /etc/sudoers.d/CUSTOM_FILE Use the following example to build the CUSTOM_FILE in the /etc/sudoers.d directory to allow any administrator belonging to a designated sudoers admin group to elevate their SELinux context with the use of the sudo command: %wheel ALL=(ALL) TYPE=sysadm_t ROLE=sysadm_r ALL
vulnerability.recommendation: Review and remediate: Elevate The SELinux Context When An Administrator Calls The Sudo Command

triage.reason: The change involves adding a standard sudoers rule to elevate SELinux context for administrators, a common STIG hardening task that does not risk lockout or service disruption.
triage.estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected

execution_details.stdout: %wheel ALL=(ALL) TYPE=sysadm_t ROLE=sysadm_r ALL
/etc/sudoers.d/selinux_sudo: parsed OK

remediation.scan_output: Vulnerability selinux_context_elevation_for_sudo not found in single-rule scan (possibly fixed)

llm_verdict.message:
The SELinux context elevation rule for the wheel group has been successfully added to `/etc/sudoers.d/selinux_sudo`. The file now contains:

```
%wheel ALL=(ALL) TYPE=sysadm_t ROLE=sysadm_r ALL
```

The configuration has been validated with `visudo -cf`, confirming syntax correctness, and the file permissions are set to `0440`. No other system services have been affected.

all_attempts.scan_output: Vulnerability selinux_context_elevation_for_sudo not found in single-rule scan (possibly fixed)

llm_verdict.message:
The SELinux context elevation rule for the wheel group has been successfully added to `/etc/sudoers.d/selinux_sudo`. The file now contains:

```
%wheel ALL=(ALL) TYPE=sysadm_t ROLE=sysadm_r ALL
```

The configuration has been validated with `visudo -cf`, confirming syntax correctness, and the file permissions are set to `0440`. No other system services have been affected.

review_verdict.feedback: The plan will add the correct SELinux type/role rule for administrators, preserve existing entries, and validate syntax, effectively addressing the vulnerability.
concerns: sed pattern '^#\s*%wheel' may not match comments with leading spaces or different formatting, potentially leaving stale commented lines.
Using plain `echo` to append might create duplicate rule lines if the file already contains a variant or partially matches the new rule; the check relies on exact string match.
If the file existed but was empty, the permission change and write may lose intended default sudoers formatting or other configurations, though rare.
The plan assumes root privileges and that `visudo -cf` can validate a single file, but `visudo` may parse all sudoers files, requiring correct file location and formatting.

qa_result.verdict_reason: The remediation plan modifies only the specific sudoers.d file, preserves existing entries, checks syntax with visudo, and ensures correct file permissions. It does not interfere with SSH, system startup, or other services, and adds the required SELinux context rule for wheel members. The risk of functionally breaking critical services is negligible.
side_effects: Possible duplicate rule if rule variant exists with different spacing; very unlikely to affect functionality.

vulnerability.title: Ensure SELinux State is Enforcing
vulnerability.description: The SELinux state should be set to at system boot time. In the file /etc/selinux/config, add or correct the following line to configure the system to boot into enforcing mode: SELINUX= Ensure that all files have correct SELinux labels by running: fixfiles onboot Then reboot the system.
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q kernel-core; then var_selinux_state='' if [ -e "/etc/selinux/config" ] ; then LC_ALL=C sed -i "/^SELINUX=/Id" "/etc/selinux/config" else touch "/etc/selinux/config" fi # make sure file has newline at the end sed -i -e '$a\' "/etc/selinux/config" cp "/etc/selinux/config" "/etc/selinux/config.bak" # Insert at the end of the file printf '%s\n' "SELINUX=$var_selinux_state" >> "/etc/selinux/config" # Clean up after ourselves. rm "/etc/selinux/config.bak" fixfiles onboot else >&2 echo 'Remediation is not applicable, nothing was done' fi

triage.reason: Changing the SELinux state to enforcing is a standard configuration change that does not lock out users or break critical services. It follows a typical STIG hardening pattern and is safe to apply.
triage.estimated_impact: reboot required; authentication/authorization affected; Requires system reboot to take effect; A misconfiguration could temporarily reduce security enforcement

execution_details.stdout:
# This file controls the state of SELinux on the system.
# SELINUX= can take one of these three values:
#     enforcing - SELinux security policy is enforced.
#     permissive - SELinux prints warnings instead of enforcing.
#     disabled - No SELinux policy is loaded.
# See also:
# https://access.redhat.com/documentation/en-us/red_hat_enterprise_linux/9/html/using_selinux/changing-selinux-states-and-modes_using-selinux#changing-selinux-modes-at-boot-time_changing-selinux-states-and-modes
#
# NOTE: Up to RHEL 8 release included, SELINUX=disabled would also
# fully disable SELinux during boot. If you need a system with SELinux
# fully disabled instead of SELinux running with no policy loaded, you
# need to pass selinux=0 to the kernel command line. You can use grubby
# to persistently set the bootloader to boot with selinux=0:
#
#    grubby --update-kernel ALL --args selinux=0
#
# To revert back to SELinux enabled:
#
#    grubby --update-kernel ALL --remove-args selinux
#
SELINUX=disabled
# SELINUXTYPE= can take one of these three values:
#     targeted - Targeted processes are protected,
#     mls - Multi Level Security protection.
SELINUXTYPE=targeted

review_verdict.feedback: The script as provided leaves the SELINUX line empty because the variable var_selinux_state is initialized to an empty string. An empty SELINUX value will not enable enforcing mode and may leave SELinux in an undefined state. Therefore, the plan would not resolve the vulnerability if executed exactly as written.
concerns: var_selinux_state is set to an empty value, leading to an empty SELINUX line.
No check or setting for SELINUXTYPE, which defaults to targeted but is not explicitly specified.
The script appends the new line rather than writing it outright, which could create duplicate or trailing whitespace entries if run multiple times.
suggested_improvements: Set var_selinux_state to a concrete value such as 'enforcing' before writing the config line.
Use a forced write (e.g., printf 'SELINUX=enforcing\n' > /etc/selinux/config) after removing any existing SELINUX lines to avoid duplicates.
Ensure SELINUXTYPE is present, defaulting to 'targeted' if omitted.
Add a verification step to check that SELINUX is actually set to enforcing after the script runs.
Include an idempotent check to avoid appending multiple lines on repeated executions.

pre_approval.rejection_reason: The script as provided leaves the SELINUX line empty because the variable var_selinux_state is initialized to an empty string. An empty SELINUX value will not enable enforcing mode and may leave SELinux in an undefined state. Therefore, the plan would not resolve the vulnerability if executed exactly as written.

vulnerability.title: Uninstall vsftpd Package

vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # CAUTION: This remediation script will remove vsftpd # from the system, and may remove any packages # that depend on vsftpd. Execute this # remediation AFTER testing on a non-production # system! if rpm -q --quiet "vsftpd" ; then dnf remove -y --noautoremove "vsftpd" fi

triage.reason: Removing the vsftpd package is a standard package removal that does not affect authentication, networking, or critical system services. It follows typical STIG hardening patterns for package management and is unlikely to cause lockout or system breakage.
triage.estimated_impact: Potential removal of packages that depend on vsftpd

vulnerability.title: Prevent Unrestricted Mail Relaying
vulnerability.description: Modify the /etc/postfix/main.cf file to restrict client connections to the local network with the following command: $ sudo postconf -e 'smtpd_client_restrictions = permit_mynetworks,reject'
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q kernel-core && rpm --quiet -q postfix; then if ! grep -q ^smtpd_client_restrictions /etc/postfix/main.cf; then echo "smtpd_client_restrictions = permit_mynetworks,reject" >> /etc/postfix/main.cf else sed -i "s/^smtpd_client_restrictions.*/smtpd_client_restrictions = permit_mynetworks,reject/g" /etc/postfix/main.cf fi else >&2 echo 'Remediation is not applicable, nothing was done' fi

triage.reason: The remediation involves a single configuration change to /etc/postfix/main.cf that limits client connections to the local network. This is a standard STIG hardening step for a non-critical service and does not risk lockout or system breakage.
triage.estimated_impact: filesystems affected; Potential temporary mail relay disruption if misconfigured

execution_details.stdout:
# Global Postfix configuration file. This file lists only a subset
# of all parameters. For the syntax, and for a complete parameter
# list, see the postconf(5) manual page (command: "man 5 postconf").
#
# For common configuration examples, see BASIC_CONFIGURATION_README
# and STANDARD_CONFIGURATION_README. To find these documents, use
# the command "postconf html_directory readme_directory", or go to
# http://www.postfix.org/BASIC_CONFIGURATION_README.html etc.
#
# For best results, change no more than 2-3 parameters at a time,
# and test if Postfix still works after every change.

# COMPATIBILITY
#
# The compatibility_level determines what default settings Postfix
# will use for main.cf and master.cf settings. These defaults will
# change over time.
#
# To avoid breaking things, Postfix will use backwards-compatible
# default settings and log where it uses those old backwards-compatible
# default settings, until the system administrator has determined
# if any backwards-compatible default settings need to be made
# permanent in main.cf or master.cf.
#
# When this review is complete, update the compatibility_level setting
# below as recommended in the RELEASE_NOTES file.
#
# The level below is what should be used with new (not upgrade) installs.
#
compatibility_level = 2

# SOFT BOUNCE
#
# The soft_bounce parameter provides a limited safety net for
# testing.  When soft_bounce is enabled, mail will remain queued that
# would otherwise bounce. This parameter disables locally-generated
# bounces, and prevents the SMTP server from rejecting mail permanently
# (by changing 5xx replies into 4xx replies). However, soft_bounce
# is no cure for address rewriting mistakes or mail routing mistakes.
#
#soft_bounce = no

# LOCAL PATHNAME INFORMATION
#
# The queue_directory specifies the location of the Postfix queue.
# This is also the root directory of Postfix daemons that run chrooted.
# See the files in examples/chroot-setup for setting up Postfix chroot
# environments on different UNIX systems.
#
queue_directory = /var/spool/postfix

# The command_directory parameter specifies the location of all
# postXXX commands.
#
command_directory = /usr/sbin

# The daemon_directory parameter specifies the location of all Postfix
# daemon programs (i.e. programs listed in the master.cf file). This
# directory must be owned by root.
#
daemon_directory = /usr/libexec/postfix

# The data_directory parameter specifies the location of Postfix-writable
# data files (caches, random numbers). This directory must be owned
# by the mail_owner account (see below).
#
data_directory = /var/lib/postfix

# QUEUE AND PROCESS OWNERSHIP
#

remediation.scan_output: Vulnerability postfix_prevent_unrestricted_relay not found in single-rule scan (possibly fixed)

llm_verdict.message:
**Remediation applied**

- `postconf -e 'smtpd_client_restrictions = permit_mynetworks,reject'` set the required directive.
- Postfix was started and reloaded; it is now active.

Run a verification scan to confirm the `postfix_prevent_unrestricted_relay` finding is resolved.

llm_verdict.message:
**Remediation applied**

- `postconf -e 'smtpd_client_restrictions = permit_mynetworks,reject'` set the required directive.
- Postfix was started and reloaded; it is now active.

Run a verification scan to confirm the `postfix_prevent_unrestricted_relay` finding is resolved.

review_verdict.feedback: The plan correctly sets smtpd_client_restrictions to permit_mynetworks,reject and reloads Postfix, thereby solving the vulnerability. It avoids manual sed edits and reduces risk of duplicate lines. Minor improvements can be made for cleanliness and verification.
concerns: If a commented smtpd_client_restrictions line exists, it will remain in main.cf but has no effect – the active line added by postconf will take precedence. Leaving the comment may clutter the file.  The plan does not explicitly check the exit status of systemctl reload, so an error during reload could go unnoticed.  The plan also does not verify that Postfix starts successfully with the new configuration or that file ownership/permissions remain unchanged.
suggested_improvements: Add a step to remove any commented #smtpd_client_restrictions lines after the postconf command to keep the file clean.  Add error handling: capture the exit status of systemctl reload and fail the script if it is non‑zero.  Run postconf -n to validate the configuration syntax before reloading to catch parse errors.  Optionally check that /etc/postfix/main.cf ownership and permissions are still root:root and 644 after modification.

qa_result.verdict_reason: The proposed plan correctly sets the smtpd_client_restrictions directive using the idempotent postconf tool, which avoids duplicate lines and preserves other configuration. Reloading Postfix applies the change safely; it only affects the mail service and does not interfere with SSH, networking, or other critical services. The remaining commented configuration lines are benign and do not affect functionality. The risk is minimal, limited to potential Postfix reload failure requiring manual intervention.
side_effects: Commented smtpd_client_restrictions lines may remain in /etc/postfix/main.cf leading to a small file clutter.
A failed systemctl reload could leave Postfix in a non-operational state, resulting in mail service interruption (no impact on SSH or other services).

vulnerability.title: The Chronyd service is enabled
vulnerability.description: chrony is a daemon which implements the Network Time Protocol (NTP) is designed to synchronize system clocks across a variety of systems and use a source that is highly accurate. More information on chrony can be found at https://chrony-project.org/. Chrony can be configured to be a client and/or a server. To enable Chronyd service, you can run: # systemctl enable chronyd.service This recommendation only applies if chrony is in use on the system.
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q kernel-core && { rpm --quiet -q chrony; }; then SYSTEMCTL_EXEC='/usr/bin/systemctl' "$SYSTEMCTL_EXEC" unmask 'chronyd.service' if [[ $("$SYSTEMCTL_EXEC" is-system-running) != "offline" ]]; then "$SYSTEMCTL_EXEC" start 'chronyd.service' fi "$SYSTEMCTL_EXEC" enable 'chronyd.service' else >&2 echo 'Remediation is not applicable, nothing was done' fi

triage.reason: Enabling the chronyd service is a standard configuration change that does not affect authentication, authorization, or critical system services. It follows typical STIG hardening patterns for service enablement and is unlikely to cause lockout or system breakage.
triage.estimated_impact: networking affected

vulnerability.title: A remote time server for Chrony is configured
vulnerability.description: Chrony is a daemon which implements the Network Time Protocol (NTP). It is designed to synchronize system clocks across a variety of systems and use a source that is highly accurate. More information on chrony can be found at https://chrony-project.org/. Chrony can be configured to be a client and/or a server. Add or edit server or pool lines to /etc/chrony.conf as appropriate: server <remote-server> Multiple servers may be configured.
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q kernel-core && { rpm --quiet -q chrony; }; then var_multiple_time_servers='' config_file="/etc/chrony.conf" if ! grep -q '^[[:space:]]*\(server\|pool\)[[:space:]]\+[[:graph:]]\+' "$config_file" ; then if ! grep -q '#[[:space:]]*server' "$config_file" ; then for server in $(echo "$var_multiple_time_servers" | tr ',' '\n') ; do printf '\nserver %s' "$server" >> "$config_file" done else sed -i 's/#[ \t]*server/server/g' "$config_file" fi if [[ -s "$config_file" ]] && [[ -n "$(tail -c 1 -- "$config_file" || true)" ]]; then LC_ALL=C sed -i --follow-symlinks '$a'\\ "$config_file" fi fi else >&2 echo 'Remediation is not applicable, nothing was done' fi

triage.reason: Adding or editing server/pool lines in /etc/chrony.conf is a standard configuration change that does not affect authentication, authorization, or critical system services. It simply ensures the system synchronizes time with a reliable NTP source.
triage.estimated_impact: networking affected; time synchronization accuracy

review_verdict.feedback: The sed command is syntactically incorrect and will fail; as a result the remedial action may not run, leaving the vulnerability unresolved.
concerns: Inaccurate sed syntax /no match detection logic
Potential creation of duplicate entries if executed
No verification that chrony service is restarted after config change
suggested_improvements: Replace sed with a reliable idempotent pattern: `grep -qE '^[[:space:]]*server[[:space:]]+time\.nist\.gov' /etc/chrony.conf || echo 'server time.nist.gov iburst' >> /etc/chrony.conf`
After modifying /etc/chrony.conf, reload chrony with `systemctl restart chronyd` or reload via `chronyc reload`
Add comment lines explaining the change for audit purposes

pre_approval.rejection_reason: The sed command is syntactically incorrect and will fail; as a result the remedial action may not run, leaving the vulnerability unresolved.

vulnerability.title: Configure Time Service Maxpoll Interval
vulnerability.description: The maxpoll should be configured to in /etc/ntp.conf or /etc/chrony.conf (or /etc/chrony.d/) to continuously poll time servers. To configure maxpoll in /etc/ntp.conf or /etc/chrony.conf (or /etc/chrony.d/) add the following after each server, pool or peer entry: maxpoll to server directives. If using chrony, any pool directives should be configured too.
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q kernel-core && { ( rpm --quiet -q chrony || rpm --quiet -q ntp ); }; then var_time_service_set_maxpoll='' pof="/usr/sbin/pidof" CONFIG_FILES="/etc/ntp.conf" $pof ntpd || { CHRONY_D_PATH=/etc/chrony.d/ mapfile -t CONFIG_FILES < <(find ${CHRONY_D_PATH}.* -type f -name '*.conf') CONFIG_FILES+=(/etc/chrony.conf) } # get list of ntp files for config_file in "${CONFIG_FILES[@]}" ; do # Set maxpoll values to var_time_service_set_maxpoll sed -i "s/^\(\(server\|pool\|peer\).*maxpoll\) [0-9,-][0-9]*\(.*\)$/\1 $var_time_service_set_maxpoll \3/" "$config_file" done for config_file in "${CONFIG_FILES[@]}" ; do # Add maxpoll to server, pool or peer entries without maxpoll grep "^\(server\|pool\|peer\)" "$config_file" | grep -v maxpo

triage.reason: Adjusting the maxpoll interval in ntp/chrony configuration is a straightforward, non‑disruptive change that does not affect authentication, authorization, or critical system services. It simply modifies how often the time service polls its servers and can be applied without risk of lockout or system breakage.

review_verdict.feedback: The current sed command replaces "maxpoll 10" with "(maxpoll) 17", introducing parentheses that break chrony syntax. Additionally, only /etc/chrony.conf is updated, so other included files (chrony.d/*) may remain non‑compliant. The plan also omits restarting or reloading chrony to apply the changes.
concerns: Incorrect sed regex adds parentheses, producing invalid configuration.
Scope limited to /etc/chrony.conf; may miss include files in /etc/chrony.d/.
No service restart/ reload step to apply changes; changes may never take effect.
No validation or idempotent check after edit.
Potential risk of time sync disruption if configuration fails to parse.
suggested_improvements: Correct the replacement regex to: sed -i -E 's/(maxpoll)[[:space:]]+[0-9]+/maxpoll 17/g'
Iterate over all files in /etc/chrony.d/ as well as /etc/chrony.conf
After edits, run 'chronyc tracking' or 'systemctl restart chronyd' to apply changes
Add a verification step to confirm config parses: chronyc tracking; if errors, revert
Handle commented lines and whitespace properly
Document the 17 value source (policy) and log the changes

pre_approval.rejection_reason: The current sed command replaces "maxpoll 10" with "(maxpoll) 17", introducing parentheses that break chrony syntax. Additionally, only /etc/chrony.conf is updated, so other included files (chrony.d/*) may remain non‑compliant. The plan also omits restarting or reloading chrony to apply the changes.

vulnerability.title: Enable Certmap in SSSD
vulnerability.description: SSSD should be configured to verify the certificate of the user or group. To set this up ensure that section like certmap/testing.test/rule_name is setup in /etc/sssd/sssd.conf. For example [certmap/testing.test/rule_name] matchrule =<SAN>.*EDIPI@mil maprule = (userCertificate;binary={cert!bin}) domains = testing.test
vulnerability.recommendation: Review and remediate: Enable Certmap in SSSD

triage.reason: Enabling certmap in SSSD is a standard configuration change that does not affect critical system components or boot process. It follows typical STIG hardening patterns for authentication services and is unlikely to lock out users if applied correctly.
triage.estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Misconfiguration may cause authentication failures for users or groups

review_verdict.feedback: The plan addresses the major concerns from previous reviews, correctly backing up configuration, checking for an existing stanza, appending with proper escaping, and restarting SSSD. It could be improved by adding a more robust validation step (e.g., `sssd --test-attribute`), ensuring quoted here‑document or single‑quoted printf to avoid unintended interpolation, and checking for any existing certmap stanza for the same domain rather than just the exact rule name.
concerns: Duplicate insertion if multiple certmap stanzas for the same domain exist; check should be more generic.
Unescaped special characters in matchrule or maprule may still cause parsing issues if quoting is insufficient.
No explicit validation of config syntax before restart.
Potential permission issues when writing to /etc/sssd/sssd.conf without sudo context.
suggested_improvements: Use `grep -q` with proper regex to detect any certmap block for the target domain, not just exact rule.
Wrap the `printf` block in single quotes (`printf '...[certmap block]...' >> /etc/sssd/sssd.conf`).
After restart, run `sssd --test-attribute` or `systemctl status sssd` to confirm the file parses correctly.
Add `set -e` at script start or check exit status of backup command to avoid silent failures.

An incorrectly formed stanza could cause SSSD to fail to start, leading to authentication failures via PAM and potentially locking out SSH access. Risk of syntax error in sssd.conf

llm_verdict.message: **Success** – auditd service is now enabled and running.

review_verdict.feedback: The plan will effectively enable and start auditd, satisfying the vulnerability requirement while incorporating idempotent checks. It could be streamlined by using systemctl enable --now, verifying systemd status, and checking existing unit existence, but those are only refinements.
concerns: No check that systemd is active before using systemctl commands; risk on non-systemd or offline systems.
Manual symlink creation bypasses systemctl enable, which might miss additional unit configuration steps.
Assumes the unit file path is always /usr/lib/systemd/system/auditd.service; may fail if path differs.
Missing explicit checks of is-enabled and is-active before attempting start, leading to unnecessary commands.
suggested_improvements: Add a guard such as 'systemctl is-system-running' or existence of /run/systemd/system to ensure systemd is active before running systemctl commands.
Use 'systemctl enable --now auditd.service' to combine enable and start in one safe, idempotent call.
Check 'systemctl is-enabled auditd.service' and 'systemctl is-active auditd.service' before creating symlink or starting the service to avoid redundant actions.
Verify that the target unit file exists before creating the symlink to avoid dangling links.
Implement error handling after systemctl daemon-reload, start, and enable commands, and log outcomes for auditability.

qa_result.verdict_reason: The remediation checks that the audit package exists, creates the standard systemd symlink if missing, reloads the daemon, and starts the auditd service—operations that are idempotent and not disruptive to critical services like sshd, networking, firewalld, or the system manager. The only minor risk is the hard‑coded unit path, but the unit is standard on Rocky Linux 10, and a missing symlink would simply mean auditd isn’t enabled. No commands that could mask or stop essential services are used, so lock‑outs or service outages are unlikely.
side_effects: No major side effects; runs only standard package and systemd commands. Potential small risk if auditd unit path is different than /usr/lib/systemd/system/auditd.service, in which case the symlink would not enable the service but would not break other services.

vulnerability.title: System Audit Logs Must Have Mode 0640 or Less Permissive
vulnerability.description: Determine where the audit logs are stored with the following command: $ sudo grep -iw log_file /etc/audit/auditd.conf log_file = /var/log/audit/audit.log Configure the audit log to be protected from unauthorized read access by setting the correct permissive mode with the following command: $ sudo chmod 0600 audit_log_file By default, audit_log_file is "/var/log/audit/audit.log".
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q audit && rpm --quiet -q kernel-core; then if LC_ALL=C grep -iw ^log_file /etc/audit/auditd.conf; then FILE=$(awk -F "=" '/^log_file/ {print $2}' /etc/audit/auditd.conf | tr -d ' ') else FILE="/var/log/audit/audit.log" fi chmod 0600 $FILE else >&2 echo 'Remediation is not applicable, nothing was done' fi

triage.reason: Changing the permissions of the audit log file to 0600 is a standard STIG hardening action that does not affect authentication, networking, or critical services. It is a simple file permission change with no risk of lockout or system breakage.

llm_verdict.message: The audit log file permissions have been set to **0600** as required.

concerns: If the audit log file does not exist at execution time, chmod will fail and the plan will leave the file unprotected.
The plan does not account for eventual log rotation or recreation, which may restore more permissive defaults.
No initial check is performed to verify that the auditd service and kernel components are present.
The plan applies the permission change only once, lacking ongoing enforcement.
suggested_improvements: Add a pre‑check for the presence of the auditd package and kernel-core before attempting permission changes.
Verify that the identified log file exists; if not, log a warning or create the file with the correct permissions.
Implement a periodic or systemd unit that re‑applies mode 0600 after log rotation or recreation to maintain compliance.
Consider capturing the effective mode again after the chmod command to confirm success.
Optional: Record the action to an audit log for future reference.

qa_result.verdict_reason: The remediation correctly sets the audit log file permissions to 0600, which satisfies the requirement that logs be at most 0640 permissive. The operation involves only reading a configuration file and applying a chmod to an existing file; it does not modify service configs or restart services. The only minor risk is if the file does not exist at the moment of execution, in which case chmod would fail, but this does not cause service disruption or security degradation. Overall, the fix is safe and effective.
side_effects: Potential chmod failure if the audit log file does not exist at execution time, leaving the file unprotected until recreated.
services_affected: auditd (may need to be restarted if permissions are changed while it is writing to the file, but chmod does not restart the service).
